coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have many new and interesting phenomena, and the concentration dependent luminescence wavelength is an intriguing one. Herein, nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) diluted to different concentrations, the high concentration of G-NSCDs (0.2 mg mL−1) with green fluorescence and the low-concentration of B-NSCDs (0.01 mg mL−1) with blue fluorescence were obtained. Further, it was found that the detection behaviors are different for different concentrations. The “turn-off” fluorescent sensor of G-NSCDs can specifically recognize rifampicin in a linear range of 0.2–20 μM with a detection limit of 56.6 nM employing the mutual effect of photo-induced electron transfer and dynamic quenching. Meanwhile, a “turn-off” B-NSCDs fluorescence sensor was constructed to effectively identify morin based on the internal filtration effect and static quenching, achieving a suitable linearity of 0.2–30 μM and a detection limit of 51.2 nM. Due to the strong chelation of morin and Al3+, a “turn-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection Al3+ was fabricated based on B-NSCDs-morin system, obtaining a good linearity of 0.1–2.0 μM with a satisfactory detection limit of 45.8 nM. These interesting behaviors indicate that NSCDs are expected to become novel sensing materials for the monitoring of rifampicin, morin and Al3+ in organisms, which is beneficial for our health.

head.ce:title: Concentration-regulated multi-color fluorescent carbon dots for the detection of rifampicin, morin and Al3 +

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have many new and interesting phenomena, and the concentration dependent luminescence wavelength is an intriguing one. Herein, nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) diluted to different concentrations, the high concentration of G-NSCDs (0.2 mg mL−1) with green fluorescence and the low-concentration of B-NSCDs (0.01 mg mL−1) with blue fluorescence were obtained. Further, it was found that the detection behaviors are different for different concentrations. The “turn-off” fluorescent sensor of G-NSCDs can specifically recognize rifampicin in a linear range of 0.2–20 μM with a detection limit of 56.6 nM employing the mutual effect of photo-induced electron transfer and dynamic quenching. Meanwhile, a “turn-off” B-NSCDs fluorescence sensor was constructed to effectively identify morin based on the internal filtration effect and static quenching, achieving a suitable linearity of 0.2–30 μM and a detection limit of 51.2 nM. Due to the strong chelation of morin and Al3+, a “turn-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection Al3+ was fabricated based on B-NSCDs-morin system, obtaining a good linearity of 0.1–2.0 μM with a satisfactory detection limit of 45.8 nM. These interesting behaviors indicate that NSCDs are expected to become novel sensing materials for the monitoring of rifampicin, morin and Al3+ in organisms, which is beneficial for our health.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tuberculosis (TB), a chronic respiratory infection, is one of the top ten causes of death in the world. The Global Tuberculosis Report published by The World Health Organization shows that there were approximately 10 million new-onset tuberculosis patients worldwide in 2019, and about 1.41 million patients died of tuberculosis [1]. Rifampicin, a broad-spectrum antibiotic, is an irreplaceable leading-edge anti-tuberculosis drug recommended by the World Health Organization. However, long-term or overdose of rifampicin can cause side effects such as joint pain, allergic rash and nervous system [2–5], and even result in serious adverse reactions such as liver damage and liver necrosis [6]. In recent years, antibiotic abuse has led to environmental hazards and physical health problems worldwide. Therefore, monitoring the content of rifampicin in water systems and biological samples plays an important role in controlling environmental pollution and reducing the risk of side effects. Morin, a flavonoid compound [7], possesses a variety of pharmacological activities, which has been clinically used to treat infections caused by viruses, gastric disease, chronic inflammation, and coronary heart disease [8–11]. In addition, morin also has significant anti-cancer properties, which inhibits DNA synthesis by destroying or preventing the reproduction of cancer cell lines and intervening cell signal transduction. Morin has been applied clinically to the adjuvant treatment of various cancers, including colon cancer [12], liver cancer [13], breast cancer [14], and so on. With the rapid development of industry, aluminum has penetrated all aspects of people's lives [15–17]. Excess aluminum in the body can cause central nervous system diseases such as Parkinson's disease [18], Alzheimer's disease [19] and memory decline [20]. Aluminum also affects and inhibits the body's hematopoietic and immune function [21,22]. Therefore, the development of a convenient and agile analytical method for the detection of morin and Al3+ is of tremendous significance in organisms (see Scheme 1 ).

At present, there are many methods for the detection of rifampicin, morin and Al3+, including spectrophotometry [23], chemiluminescence [24], electrochemistry [25–27], liquid chromatography [28–30], and fluorescence [31–45]. Among them, fluorescence spectroscopy has aroused the great interests of researchers due to its advantages of high sensitivity, superior selectivity, convenient operation, and rapid response. In 2004, carbon dots (CDs) appeared for the first time [46], as a new generation of fluorescent materials, which have provided important applications in many fields, including biomedicine, catalysis, and optoelectronic devices because of their excellent tunable photoluminescence, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and abundant low-cost raw materials [39,45,47–49]. So far, there are some CDs for the detection of rifampicin, morin, and Al3+. In 2018, Han et al. [31] used ammonium citrate as inorganic carbon source and ammonium thiocyanate as a nitrogen source to synthesize CDs with blue emission and detected rifampicin selectively and sensitively based on the internal filtration effect. However, ammonium thiocyanate has toxicity to a certain degree, which limits its application range. Al-Hashimi et al. [32] employed ciprofloxacin as the raw material and concentrated phosphoric acid as the solvent and constructed a one-step solvothermal method to prepare CNDs for rifampicin detection. Although it had a relatively low detection limit (60 nM), phosphoric acid with pungent odor was used in the preparation process, which would harm to human body and pollute the environment. Li et al. [36] prepared CDs with citric acid and polyethyleneimine (PEI), which had both up-conversion and down-conversion photo-luminescence properties, and produced a sensitive response to morin. However, the detection limit obtained was relatively high (0.6 μM), which made it not widely used. Li et al. [37] prepared N and S co-doped fluorescent CDs to detect morin and obtained a lower detection limit (56 nM), but it is still not as sensitive as the prepared B-NSCDs in this work (LOD = 51.2 nM). Wang et al. [17] achieved high-sensitivity fluorescence detection of Al3+ by visualization and ratio in the RCDs-QCT system. However, the toxic reagents including p-phenylenediamine and toluene were used in the synthesis process of RCDs, which caused pollution and harmed to the environment. Gui et al. [41] reported a “Turn-On” fluorescent probe for detecting Al3+ in cells. The probe had excellent sensitivity (LOD = 21.6 nM), but the synthesis steps were cumbersome. Therefore, it is imminent to design a fluorescent probe that is convenient, fast, sensitive, environmentally friendly, and with a low detection limit to sequentially detect rifampicin, morin and Al3+ with multi-mode detection mechanisms.

The concentration dependent luminescence wavelength is an interesting phenomenon, while there are few articles on it. It was reported that carbon dots with concentration-tunable multicolored photoluminescence detected simultaneously on Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions. Their photoluminescence (PL) gradually evolved three strongest peaks with concentration reduction, and these strongest PL peaks exhibited different response patterns for Fe3+ and Cu2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity [50].

Herein, it was found that the detection behaviors are different for different concentrations. The “turn-off” fluorescent sensor of G-NSCDs can specifically recognize rifampicin in a linear range of 0.2–20 μM with a detection limit of 56.6 nM employing the mutual effect of photo-induced electron transfer and dynamic quenching. Meanwhile, a “turn-off” B-NSCDs fluorescence sensor was constructed to effectively identify morin based on the internal filtration effect and static quenching, achieving a suitable linearity of 0.2–30 μM and a detection limit of 51.2 nM. Due to the strong chelation of morin and Al3+, a “turn-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection Al3+ was fabricated based on B-NSCDs-morin system, obtaining a good linearity of 0.1–2.0 μM with a satisfactory detection limit of 45.8 nM. Simultaneously, the probe had also been successfully applied for trace analysis of target substances in actual samples with accurate and satisfactory results. Compared with the reported analysis methods, the constructed sensors show superior fluorescence performances and have broad prospects in the fields of disease diagnosis, biosensors, and water quality monitoring. These interesting phenomena open a door for the exploration new materials with novel potentials.

Rifampicin, morin, quinine sulfate, amino acids and other drugs were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The reagents for the metal ions and anions were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). The 0.1 M PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.0, Na2HPO4–NaH2PO4) was prepared. A standard stock solution of 0.005 M Al3+ was prepared with aluminum nitrate. All reagents used in the experiment were analytical reagents and used directly without further purification. The experimental water was ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1).

The morphology of NSCDs were obtained by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The fluorescence spectrum was detected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS-55, Perkin Elmer, USA), the excitation and emission slit width was both 10.0 nm, and the scanning speed was 800 nm min−1. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of NSCDs were recorded by a Lambda 900 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was tested on the FluoroMax-4 TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by X'Pert Powder X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, The Netherlands), using Cu Kɑ as the radiation source, and the voltage and current were 40 kV and 40 mA, respectively. The Infrared spectrum (FT-IR) of NSCDs was measured on a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) with a scanning range of 500–4000 cm−1. The Zeta potential and the particle size of NSCDs were obtained by a nanometer particle size potential analyzer (Nano series-ZS90, Malvern, UK). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve was measured by IVIUM electrochemical workstation (Vertex. One. EIS, Netherlands), and the scanning speed was 50 mV s−1. The electrochemical test used platinum wire as the counter electrode, calomel electrode as the reference electrode, platinum carbon electrode as the working electrode, and tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate as the electrolyte (0.01 M, dichloromethane).

Citric acid (1.0500 g) and 1.5200 g of thiourea were added into an agate mortar with a molar ratio of 1:4. After fully grinding, the mixed powder was transferred into a 30 mL crucible and was heated at 170 °C for 3 h. After being left to cool to room temperature, the obtained black product was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The collected supernatant was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500) for 48 h to remove unreacted raw material and other impurities. And then the purified CDs powder was obtained after freeze-dried at −50 °C for 48 h. Ten mg mL−1 CDs solution were prepared and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further characterization and application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: G-NSCDs quenching fluorescence sensor for rifampicin detection
ce:section.ce:para: NSCDs (10 mg mL−1) were diluted 50 times and recorded as high-concentration G-NSCDs with a concentration of 0.2 mg mL−1. Three mL G-NSCDs (0.2 mg mL−1), 100 μL PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.0) buffer solution and 30 μL different types substances (metal ions, anionic ions, amino acids and drugs) with a concentration of 0.01 M were added into a quartz cuvette. After shaking and incubating for 1 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectrum was tested setting an excitation wavelength with 460 nm. Subsequently, rifampicin was added into a mixture of amino acids or drugs to evaluate the interference of coexisting substances on the fluorescence quenching of G-NSCDs. The sensitivity of G-NSCDs to rifampicin was carried out with fluorescence titration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of morin by B-NSCDs quenching fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: NSCDs (10 mg mL−1) were diluted 1000 times and recorded as low-concentration B-NSCDs with a concentration of 0.01 mg mL−1. Three mL B-NSCDs solution (0.01 mg mL−1) and 100 μL PBS solution were mixed in a quartz cuvette. Then, different types of amino acids or drugs were added with a final concentration of 100 μM to evaluate the specific recognition to B-NSCDs. Next, 30 μL standard solution of morin (0.005 M) was blended into the above mixed solutions and the changes of fluorescence intensity were observed at 458 nm to assess the interference of coexisting substances on the recognition of morin. Subsequently, different volumes of morin standard solution were sequentially added into the mother liquor formed by B-NSCDs and buffer solution to detect the response range and the detection limit of morin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: B-NSCDs-morin complex enhanced fluorescence sensor to detect Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Three mL of B-NSCDs solution (0.01 mg mL−1), 100 μL PBS solution and 60 μL 0.005 M morin were added into a quartz cuvette to construct a B-NSCDs-morin fluorescence-enhanced sensor for Al3+ detection. Different types of metal ions were added into the complex system to observe the changes of fluorescence intensity at 460 nm. At the same time, the sensitivity of the B-NSCDs-morin system to Al3+ was investigated using the fluorescence titration method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of rifampicin, morin and Al3+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Environmental water samples were taken from the urban area of Anshan. Urine and serum samples were taken from healthy adults in the urban hospital. Rifampicin capsules and aluminum hydroxide tablets were purchased from a pharmacy. The standard recovery experiment was used to appraise the reliability and accuracy of the NSCDs fluorescence sensor for the detection of rifampicin, morin and Al3+.

As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is applied to analyze the morphology, particle size and dispersion of NSCDs. Fig. 1A shows NSCDs are obviously spherical, uniform in size and dispersive evenly. The inset is a particle size distribution diagram obtained by statistical analysis of 100 nanoparticles at random. The average diameter of NSCDs is 3.53 nm ± 0.62 nm. From HRTEM image of NSCDs (Fig. 1B), a clear lattice stripes with a spacing distance of 0.32 nm can be seen, corresponding to the (002) lattice spacing of graphite carbon (SP2), which suggests that the prepared carbon core possessed a graphite-like structure [51–53].

Fig. 1C represents the XRD pattern of NSCDs. An obvious and broad diffraction peak at 25.1° is observed, according to the (002) diffraction peak lattice spacing (d = 0.32 nm) of the graphite carbon material. It is confirmed the synthesized NSCDs have a graphene structure, which is consistent with the above-mentioned HRTEM results [51,53,54]. From Fig. 1D, there are two typical characteristic absorption peaks at 3420 cm−1 and 3190 cm−1, indicating that NSCDs contain O–H and N–H groups [55]. The two peaks at 3030 cm−1 and 2840 cm−1 are attributed to the vibration of the -C-H bond [56]. The stretching vibration peaks at 2405 cm−1, 1210 cm−1 and 763 cm−1 are ascribed to the characteristic absorption peaks of S–H [57], C=S [58] and C–S [59], respectively. The absorption peaks centering at 1730 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 are caused by the vibrations of oxygen-containing carbon core C=O and C–O–C [58,59]. The absorption peaks at 1640 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1 indicate the existence of the stretching vibration of C=C and C–N [57]. The above results reveal the surface of NSCDs is covered with a substantial number of hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino or amide groups, indicating that N and S co-doped carbon dots have been successfully prepared. These groups endow NSCDs with extremely strong hydrophilicity, good dispersibility and stability in aqueous solutions.

The XPS spectrum of NSCDs (Fig. 2 A) shows four representative peaks around 163 eV, 285 eV, 400 eV, and 532 eV correspond to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, which confirms that nitrogen and sulfur have been doped into the CDs successfully. Fig. 2B depicted a high-resolution image of C1s. The deconvolution of C1s spectrum can be fitted into four characteristic peaks at 284.78, 285.98 eV, 287.58 eV and 288.53 eV, which are attributed to C=C/C–C, C–N/-C-S, C–O/C–O–C and C=O groups, respectively [57–59]. Three distinctive peaks at 398.73 eV, 399.98 eV and 401.28 eV in N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) indicate C–N–C, N-(C)3 and N–H groups [57]. The O1s spectra (Fig. 2D) decomposes into two peaks at 531.68 eV and 533.23 eV, which can be assigned to C=O and C–OH/C–O–C groups [56]. As for the S2p spectrum (Fig. 2E), the peaks center at 162.48 eV and 164.08 eV related to the presence of -C-S-S2p 3/2 and -C-S-S2p 1/2 [50,58], respectively. The XPS results agree well with the FT-IR analysis results. The zeta potential of 0.2 mg mL−1 and 0.01 mg mL−1 of NSCDs was −6.2 ± 0.70 mV and −1.59 ± 0.14 mV, respectively, which may be attributed to the presence of a large number of negatively charged carboxyl/hydroxyl groups on CDs surface. The existence of hydrophilic functional groups makes these nanomaterials represent excellent water-solubility. Fig. 3 displayed the maximum λex of G-NSCDs and B-NSCDs were 460 nm and 375 nm, and the corresponding maximum λem were located at 552 nm and 458 nm, respectively. According to the previous procedures [47], The quantum yield of NSCDs is estimated to be 12.03%.

To evaluate the possibility of NSCDs in practical applications, the influence of external environment on the chemical properties and optical stability of NSCDs (10 mg mL−1) diluted 50 times and 1000 times were explored. Figs. S1–A depicts the concentration of NaCl solution is as high as 2.0 M, the fluorescence intensities do not change significantly, which indicates that NSCDs can be stable in a high ionic strength environment. The fluorescence intensities of NSCDs with high and low concentration decrease in strong acid and alkali solutions (Figs. S1–B). In a strong acid environment, the surface of NSCDs is covered with rich –COOH groups, which makes it easy for hydrogen bonds to form. Protonation of carboxyl on the surface of NSCDs induces the aggregation of NSCDs and weakens the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs [60]. In a strong alkaline environment, a substantial amount of OH− interacts with H+ in the carboxyl groups to generate COO−, which covers the surface of NSCDs in abundant negative charges, destroys the surface functional groups of NSCDs, and reduces the fluorescence intensity [61]. Therefore, the physiological environment (pH = 7) is chosen as the most suitable pH for detection. The high and low concentrations of NSCDs are continuously irradiated under a UV lamp for 3 h (Figs. S1–C), and the fluorescence intensity only slightly decreases, which reveals that NSCDs have superior resistance to photobleaching. Adding different concentrations of H2O2 to NSCDs solution, Figs. S1–D manifests that NSCDs have strong antioxidant capacity. The fluorescence properties of NSCDs in complex environments are excellent compared to organic fluorescent dyes, which greatly expands the application in the fields of biomarkers and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn off” detection of rifampicin based on G-NSCDs

ce:list-item.ce:para: Selectivity and sensitivity of rifampicin detection

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S2-A and B display that only rifampicin can effectively quench the fluorescence of G-NSCDs at the same concentration of 100 μM, while the fluorescence intensities of G-NSCDs by adding metal ions, anions, amino acids and other drug molecules do not significantly change. Figs. S2–C compares the change of fluorescence signal with and without rifampicin in the coexistence of interfering substances. When rifampicin is added into the system where G-NSCDs and interfering substances coexist, the fluorescence signals obviously quench, and the quenching degree is the same as the fluorescence quenching rate of rifampicin only (about 80%). Subsequently, the concentration of interfering substances is increased to further estimate the tolerance levels of coexisting substances with an error of no more than ±5%, as shown in Table S1. The results indicate that the tolerable multiples are higher, which verify that G-NSCDs show remarkable selectivity and specific recognition for rifampicin. Figs. S2–D records that rifampicin (100 μM) is added into the G-NSCDs solution for 30 s, the fluorescence intensity decreases notably and remains unchanged, which suggests that there is a quick and sensitive response for rifampicin. Based on this, a fluorescent probe for detecting rifampicin is constructed, which is expected to be a sensitive probe for monitoring the content of rifampicin in a complex matrix.

Due to the complexity of the actual sample, in addition to investigating the selectivity of the established fluorescence analysis method, sensitivity is also another important parameter for evaluation. The sensitivity of G-NSCDs to rifampicin was explored by color comparison under UV light and fluorescence titration. With a gradual increase of the concentration of rifampicin (0–100 μM), the brown of NSCDs in daylight deepens. At the same time, the green fluorescence of G-NSCDs under ultraviolet light gradually weakens until the fluorescence quenches (Fig. 4 A). So, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of NSCDs can be visually observed through the changing of fluorescence color.

As described in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of G-NSCDs at 552 nm gradually declines with the increasing concentration of rifampicin, which reveals that G-NSCDs as a fluorescent probe is very sensitive to the detection of rifampicin. Within the entire responsive range (0–100 μM), ΔF (ΔF=F–F0, where F0 and F represent the emission intensity of G-NSCDs at 552 nm in the absence and presence of rifampicin, respectively) and rifampicin concentrations cannot be fitted linearly (Fig. 4C). However, the fluorescence decay ΔF is linear with the concentration of rifampicin in the range of 0.2–20 μM. The fitted equation is ΔF = 14.84 C rifampicin + 19.24, and R2 is 0.9911. According to 3σ/k, the detection limit is 56.6 nM. Where σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank samples and k is the slope of the calibration curve. The fluorescence analysis method we established is compared with the previous analysis method for rifampicin (Table S2). So, the G-NSCDs fluorescence quenching sensor fabricated by adjusting concentration has the advantages of low price of raw materials, simple preparation, low detection limit, and environmental friendliness, which can be used for label-free detection of rifampicin in actual environmental water samples and complex biological samples.
(2)
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of G-NSCDs for rifampicin detection

The UV spectrum, fluorescence spectrum, Zeta potential, cyclic voltammetry curve (CV) and fluorescence decay curve were utilized to explore the quenching mechanism of G-NSCDs for the determination of rifampicin. As described in Fig. 5 A, the UV absorption spectrum of rifampicin near 460 nm overlaps with the G-NSCDs’ absorption spectra, which speculates that the possible fluorescence quenching mechanisms may be caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [62,63]. If FRET can occur between the excited G-NSCDs and the ground state quencher rifampicin, the condition that needs to be met is the fluorescence lifetime of G-NSCDs will be decayed after adding the quencher. Therefore, the double exponential fluorescence decay curves were fitted according to formula
T
( t )
=
A 1
exp

− t /

)
+
A 2
exp
(
− t /
τ 2
)
(Fig. 5B).

Where A 1 and A 2 are the corresponding amplitudes,
τ 1
and
τ 2
are the time coefficients of the decay curve. The average fluorescence lifetime of G-NSCDs with and without 20 μM rifampicin was calculated to be 5.54 ns and 6.35 ns, respectively. That is, energy transfer or electron transfer may occur between G-NSCDs and rifampicin. The zeta potentials of G-NSCDs and rifampicin (5 mM) were measured to be −6.2 ± 0.70 mV and −2.34 ± 0.33 mV, respectively. The distance between G-NSCDs and rifampicin is more than 10 nm because the electrostatic attraction between them is weak, which confirms that the fluorescence quenching is not caused by FRET [64]. Concurrently, IFE and static quenching mechanisms are also excluded, because these two quenching mechanisms require the fluorescence lifetime of G-NSCDs with and without rifampicin not alter [62–64].

Next, the electrochemical properties of G-NSCDs were characterized using cyclic voltammetry to further verify whether it was the fluorescence quenching caused by photo-induced electron transfer (PET). The highest occupied orbit (HOMO) energy level and the lowest unoccupied orbit (LUMO) energy level of G-NSCDs were calculated according to empirical formulas
E

)

)
[62,63,65].

Where E ox and E red represent the oxidation potential and the reduction potential of G-NSCDs, respectively. From Fig. 5C, E red of G-NSCDs is −0.56 eV, and the E LUMO of G-NSCDs is calculated to be −3.84 eV. Due to the irreversibility of the oxidation process, the E HOMO of G-NSCDs cannot be directly calculated by Equation 3. Based on the UV spectra of G-NSCDs, the forbidden band width E
g is measured to be 2.63 eV (inset), and then the E HOMO of G-NSCDs is estimated to be −6.47 eV by formula

E

[65]. Similarly, the E HOMO and E LUMO of rifampicin are also tested to be −6.70 eV and −3.92 eV, respectively (Fig. 5D).

As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, the excited state electrons of G-NSCDs can be transferred from the LUMO energy level to the LUMO energy level of rifampicin, indicating that PET causes the fluorescence quenching of G-NSCDs. Fig. 6B represents the excitation and emission spectra of rifampicin, which provide the support for the proposed model.

Fig. 5B signifies that the fluorescence lifetime of G-NSCDs has strikingly changed with adding and without adding rifampicin, which speculates that dynamic quenching may also occur. The quenching type is determined by the Stern-Volmer formula
F 0
/ F = 1 +
k
s v
[ Q ]
[66]. Where K sv represents the quenching constant of G-NSCDs. [Q] is the concentration of rifampicin.

Dynamic quenching refers to the collision between the molecule in the excited state of G-NSCDs and the rifampicin molecule. Energy transfer or charge transfer occurs during the collision, so the excitation energy weakens or even disappears, resulting in the quenching of its fluorescence. The molecular collisions intensify with the temperature rising, which lead to the quenching increasing. Since only the collision occurs, the excited state is not affected, and the UV spectrum of G-NSCDs will not change [67]. To further confirm whether dynamic quenching had occurred, the relationship between K sv and the different temperature of the G-NSCDs-rifampicin system at 298 K, 318 K, and 328 K (Fig. 7 A) was explored. Noticeably, K sv shows an upward trend with the increase of temperature (Table S3), which also certifies that the quenching of G-NSCDs by rifampicin conforms to the dynamic quenching. As presented in Fig. 7B, when different concentrations of rifampicin (0–100 μM) are added into the G-NSCDs solution, the UV absorption intensities of G-NSCDs at 251 nm, 342 nm and 456 nm gradually increase, but there is no other absorption peak. In summary, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of G-NSCDs on rifampicin is the result of the synergistic effect of PET and dynamic quenching.

At present, some livestock and poultry farmers take antibiotics as a panacea and add blindly in large doses, which are ingested by the human body through the food chain. The abuse of antibiotics has caused irreparable damage to the environment and physical health. So, the rifampicin content in environmental water, urine and serum of healthy person by standard recovery was determined (Table 1 ). The measured recovery rate is between 95.6 and 104.6%, and a relatively low RSD between 1.69 and 3.98% is obtained. At the same time, rifampicin was also detected in rifampicin capsules by the same fluorescent sensors. After adding 5 μM and 10 μM rifampicin standard solution, a lower RSD and satisfactory recoveries are achieved. The results demonstrate the constructed G-NSCDs fluorescence sensor can be a strong candidate for detecting rifampicin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn off” detection of morin based on B-NSCDs

ce:list-item.ce:para: The assessment of selectivity and sensitivity of B-NSCDs to morin

ce:section.ce:para: As observed from Fig. 8 A, with the increase of the morin concentration (0–100 μM), the color of B-NSCDs solution darkens under sunlight, meanwhile the blue fluorescence gradually quenches under the UV light, signifying that the morin content can be visually monitored. Simultaneously, the emission intensity of B-NSCDs at 460 nm appears a downward trend (Fig. 8B), and the fluorescence is almost completely quenched at 100 μM, which shows that B-NSCDs have excellent sensitivity to morin.

In the concentration range (0.2–30 μM), ΔF and morin concentration can form a good linear relationship (Fig. 8C). The linear equation is ΔF = 14.66 C morin+36.13 (ΔF=F0–F, F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of B-NSCDs at 460 nm with and without addition of morin), and the correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9921. According to the formula LOD = 3σ/k, the detection limit of the method is 51.2 nM. Comparison of analytical methods for detection of morin reported in the literature (Table S5), the low-concentration B-NSCDs constructed by adjusting the concentration is completely comparable to the existing morin sensors. It has the brilliant advantages of high selectivity, low detection limit, and superior sensitivity, which is beneficial to realize real-time, rapid and label-free detection of morin in actual samples.
(2)
The fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-NSCDs to morin

As noticed clearly from Fig. 9 A, the UV spectrum of morin near 360 nm overlaps the fluorescence excitation spectrum of B-NSCDs to a certain extent, speculating that the fluorescence quenching of B-NSCDs may be caused by FRET or IFE. Assuming that the fluorescence quenching of B-NSCDs is caused by FRET, it is necessary to further explore whether the fluorescence lifetime changes with and without of morin. Fitting the fluorescence lifetime of B-NSCDs by the double exponential fluorescence decay curve, which are 6.01 ns, 6.04 ns and 6.00 ns with addition of 0 μM, 10 μM and 30 μM of morin, respectively (Fig. 9B). The fluorescence lifetime is the same, concluding it is not FRET. Simultaneously, the Zeta potentials of morin (5 mM) and B-NSCDs were determined to be −12.06 ± 0.58 mV and −1.59 ± 0.14 mV, respectively. Both are negatively charged, indicating that repulsive forces generate, which cause the distance between B-NSCDs and morin to be greater than 10 nm, eliminating the FRET quenching mechanism again.

Subsequently, electrochemical methods were applied to determine whether the fluorescence quenching was caused by PET. The E red and E
g are calculated to be −0.58 eV and 2.65 eV (Fig. 9C). The E LUMO and E HOMO of B-NSCDs determined by formula (3) and (4) are −3.82 eV and −6.47 eV, respectively. Similarly, E red and E
g of morin are calculated to be −0.65 eV and 2.51 eV, respectively (Fig. 9D). So, the E LUMO and E HOMO of morin are −3.75 eV and −6.26 eV, respectively.

Fig. 10 A displays that the excited state electrons of B-NSCDs cannot be transferred from the LUMO energy level to the LUMO energy level of morin. Therefore, the PET quenching mechanism is also excluded. Due to the overlapping of spectrum and unchanging of the fluorescence lifetime, it is speculated that the fluorescence quenching of B-NSCDs is caused by IFE. Fig. 10B represents the excitation and emission spectra of morin, which provides the support for the proposed model.

Next, the relationship between K sv and temperature of the B-NSCDs-morin system was shown in Fig. 11 A and Table S6. The data illustrated K sv appears a downward trend with a gradual increase of temperature, which speculates a static quenching mechanism. The reason is that a non-fluorescent complex is formed between B-NSCDs and morin, the complex gradually decomposes with the increasing of temperature, then the stability declines, and the K sv decreases. The UV spectra of different concentrations of morin (0–250 μM) adding into the B-NSCDs solution (Fig. 11B) display that the UV absorption intensity and absorption peak change significantly. The absorption peak at 335 nm gradually weakens, and new absorption peaks appeared at 295 nm and 352 nm, signifying that B-NSCDs and morin forms a new non-fluorescent ground state complex, which confirms the static quenching again. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of morin on B-NSCDs is the result of the synergistic effect of IFE and static quenching.
(3)
Practical application of B-NSCDs in detection of morin

The as-prepared probe was applied to detect the content of morin in serum and urine samples of healthy people (Table 2 ). By adding 5 μM, 15 μM and 25 μM morin to the actual samples, the spike recovery rate is in the range of 95.4%–104.4%, and the RSD is also low. It is proved that the low-concentration B-NSCDs constructed by regulating the concentration is accurate and feasible for detecting morin in actual samples and owns a splendid application prospect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn on” detection of Al3+ based on the B-NSCDs-morin complex

ce:list-item.ce:para: The selectivity and sensitivity of B-NSCDs-morin for detecting Al3+

ce:section.ce:para: Since the fluorescence intensity of B-NSCDs is completely quenched by morin, it is possible to create a “turn on” fluorescent probe to detect a new target. Figs. S4–A exhibits that zinc ion can restore the fluorescence signal to a lesser extent, while aluminum ion can almost totally recover the fluorescence, and other metal ions have no effect on the fluorescence of the complex system. The interference results in Figs. S4–B display that the coexisting metal ions can not affect the fluorescence of the B-NSCDs-morin complex system restored by Al3+. The data in Table S7 represent that the interfering ions can tolerate relatively high multiples, indicating that the B-NSCDs-morin complex can be used as a fluorescence-enhanced sensor to selectively identify Al3+. The interaction time between the complex and Al3+ (25 μM) is shown in Figs. S4–C, and the fluorescence signal recovers to the maximum and is basically stable at 2 min.

Simultaneously, the sensitivity of the complex system to Al3+ was also investigated. As seen intuitively from Fig. 12 A, under the irradiation of UV light, the fluorescent color gradually changes from weak blue to green with the concentration increasing of Al3+ (0–25 μM), denoting the process of fluorescence recovery of the B-NSCDs-morin system. The changing of color signifies that a new fluorescent substance may be generated, and the content of Al3+ can be judged by visual monitoring. Fig. 12B is the fluorescence emission spectrum of the B-NSCDs-morin system after adding different concentrations of Al3+. It can be clearly shown that the peak position shifts from 460 nm to 502 nm, and the fluorescence signal is gradually restored, and the fluorescence recovery rate is up to 85%. ΔF has a satisfying linear relationship with C Al 3+ in the range of 0.1–2.0 μM (Fig. 12C). According to the linear equation ΔF = 16.36 C Al 3+-1.59, where ΔF=F–F0, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the B-NSCDs-morin system without and with Al3+, the detection limit for detecting Al3+ is calculated as 45.8 nM (3σ/k, n = 11). Compared with the literature methods (Table S8), it testifies that the analytical method of detecting Al3+ based on the B-NSCDs-morin fluorescence-enhanced sensor is simple to operate, with a low detection limit and suitable sensitivity.

At first, the influence of different concentrations of Al3+ on the absorbance of morin is investigated (Fig. 13 A). With the increase of Al3+ concentration (1–24 μM), the characteristic absorption peak at 358 nm gradually weakens, and red-shifts to 420 nm with the absorbance gradually enhancing, assuming a new complex was formed between morin and Al3+, which leads to a redshift of the absorption peak. Subsequently, the UV absorption spectra of different concentrations of Al3+ added into the B-NSCDs-morin system was investigated (Fig. 13B). Accompanied by the concentration increase of Al3+, the UV absorption peaks of complex at 295 nm disappears, while the absorbance of the characteristic peak at the same position of 358 nm and 420 nm emerges the same change as in Fig. 13A, that is, decrease first and then increase. The fluorescence lifetimes of adding different concentrations of Al3+ (0, 10 μM and 25 μM) to the B-NSCDs-morin system are 6.00 ns, 5.96 ns and 5.94 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes do not change significantly (Fig. 13C), speculating a new complex might be formed.

Therefore, the possible reason for the fluorescence recovery of the B-NSCDs-morin system is the strong chelation between morin and Al3+ dissociates the morin from the B-NSCDs-morin system and forms morin-Al3+ complex with stronger binding force, which leads the quenched blue fluorescence to recover, and the fluorescence emission spectrum is red-shifted.

The content of Al3+ in aluminum hydroxide tablets, urine and serum of healthy people was measured by the constructed B-NSCDs-morin fluorescence sensor. As shown in Table 3 , the standard recovery of Al3+ is between 96.0% and 104.0%, and the RSD does not exceed 4%, revealing the B-NSCDs-morin fluorescence sensor can be used for the analysis of Al3+ in biological diagnosis and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of detection mechanism of G-NSCDS and B-NSCDS
ce:section.ce:para: It had been confirmed by TEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR, ESR and UV that the surface of the prepared NSCDs contained abundant functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and sulfhydryl. Due to the existence of the C=O groups, the ground state electrons of NSCDs occur n-π∗ transition. When the excited state electrons returned to the ground state, electron/hole radiative recombination is formed, which emits blue fluorescence of B-NSCDs. When rifampicin is added into B-NSCDs solution, inner filter effect is generated because of the interaction between rifampicin and surface groups of B-NSCDs, which results in the quenching of blue fluorescence. As the concentration of NSCDs increases gradually, NSCDs agglomerate by the Van der Waals force (Fig. 14 ), which results in an increase of the conjugated π domain structure, narrows the π-π∗ electron energy level, alters the charge density and bandwidth energy gap of the conjugated plane of NSCDs and then creates a new fluorescent center, obtaining green, fluorescent G-NSCDs. With the addition of morin to G-NSCDs solution, the luminescent center is destroyed due to photo-induced electron transfer, causing the quenching of the green fluorescent. Therefore, utilizing the concentration control mechanism, G-NSCDs and B-NSCDs have different surface functional groups, different particle sizes and different response target substances [68].

Inexpensive citric acid was used as the inorganic carbon source, thiourea was selected as the dopant, and the one-step solid-phase pyrolysis without solvent addition was applied to prepare NSCDs with excellent fluorescence properties. Employing characterization methods including TEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR and UV, it is proved that the surface of NSCDs is rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and sulfhydryl functional groups, which can interact with the surface groups of the target. On the basis of the concentration regulation, high-concentration G-NSCDs and low-concentration B-NSCDs are fabricated by diluting the prepared NSCDs. Based on the PET and dynamic quenching mechanism, the “turn-off” G-NSCDs fluorescence sensor is explored for sensitive detection the antibiotic rifampicin, a detection limit of 56.6 nM and satisfactory recoveries with 95.6–104.6% is achieved to determine rifampicin in environmental water, rifampicin capsules, urine and serum samples. Based on IFE and static quenching mechanism, a “turn-off” B-NSCDs fluorescence sensor was constructed to efficaciously identify morin. A good linearity (0.2–30 μM) is accomplished between ΔF and morin concentration, and the detection limit is 51.2 nM. Due to the strong chelation effect of morin and Al3+, applying the “turn-off-on” mechanism on the basis of the B-NSCDs-morin system, a well-selected and highly sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting Al3+ was fabricated. The response range of Al3+ is 0.1–2.0 μM, and the detection limit of 45.8 nM is calculated. Analyzing morin and Al3+ in aluminum hydroxide tablets, urine and serum samples was perfectly achieved. Hence, the multicolor fluorescent CDs for the detection of rifampicin, morin and Al3+ based on concentration regulation have the advantages of simplicity, rapidity, adequate selectivity, and superior sensitivity, which presents remarkable application potential in the fields of disease diagnosis, water quality detection, and biomedicine.

Xiao-dan Tang, Hong-mei Yu: Experimental design, synthesis and characterization; Shuanping Cui, Hunter Temple, and Ming-li Chen: Data analysis and cell studies; Yun Gao and Eric Amador: Data analysis, Editing and polishing; Shao-yan Wang, Zhi-zhi Hu, Wei Chen: Conceptualization, experimental design, supervision and funding; Wei Chen, Eric Amador and Xiao-dan Tang: Writing, validation and visualization.
